coredata.dc:title: Time-resolved photoluminescence of pH-sensitive carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Basu, Nabaruna
Mandal, Debabrata
coredata.dc:description: Time-resolved photoluminescence emission were performed to study the ≥100 ps time-scale photophysics of water soluble carbon dots (CDs) dispersed in different pH solutions. Excitation of the core and high-energy edge states at excitation wavelengths λex ≤ 350 nm produce hole-electron excitonic charge-carriers but do not yield any photoluminescence emission, implying efficient non-radiative recombination mechanisms for the charge-carriers. Using λex ∼375 nm, surface fluorophores as well as low-lying edge-states are excited, producing emission over a broad range. However, the emission characteristics vary strongly depending on the pH condition and the choice of excitation wavelength. In particular, the edge state emission is abruptly extinguished at pH ≥ 5, which also marks the onset of deprotonation of surface-bound -COOH groups of the CDs, indicating that the -COO- anionic centers promote quenching of edge-state emission. Using λex ∼450 nm, only the surface fluorophores are excited. The deprotonation of phenolic -OH group at pH > 9 causes a sharp red-shift in the emission spectra at all excitation wavelengths. The pH-sensitive emission behavior of the CDs enable them to serve as optical pH sensors.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support of this work was obtained from DST-SERB , India (Project No. EMR/2017/000387 ), and CSIR , India (Project No. 01(2390)/10/EMR – II ). Time-resolved picosecond spectroscopic experiments were carried out at the University of Calcutta with the TCSPC instrument purchased under the DST (FIST Program), India. N. B. thanks University Grants Commission, India, for awarding research fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM, HRTEM images and size distribution of CDs in aqueous medium. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Normalized photoluminescence emission (solid curves) and excitation spectra (dotted curves) of CDs solutions of different pH at various excitation (λex) and emission (λem) wavelengths, respectively, as indicated. The black solid curve in each panel represents the normalized absorption spectrum of the CDs solution at the corresponding pH. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Positions of Emission peaks and Isoemissive points plotted as a function of pH at,t different excitation wavelengths (λex). Dots and open circles represent the emission peaks andd isoemissive points respectively as indicated in the legend. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Photoluminescence time-profiles of CDs solutions at pH = 2, 7, 11 at different emission wavelengths. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TRANES of CDs solutions of different pH, recorded with excitation wavelengths 375 nm and 450 nm. The curves in each panel represent the TRANES at different time delays, starting from 100 ps up to 10 ns. Arrows mark the positions of isoemissive points. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Simplified diagram depicting the probable mechanism for different pH solutions of CDs due to λex = 375 and 450 nm. At λex = 375 nm, both edge (E) and surface fluorophores (S) are excited whereas at 450 nm excitation only surface fluorophores are excited. Dotted and solid arrows stand for edge state and surface state emission respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Time-resolved photoluminescence of pH-sensitive carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Calcutta, 92, APC Road, Kolkata, 700009, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Time-resolved photoluminescence emission were performed to study the ≥100 ps time-scale photophysics of water soluble carbon dots (CDs) dispersed in different pH solutions. Excitation of the core and high-energy edge states at excitation wavelengths λex ≤ 350 nm produce hole-electron excitonic charge-carriers but do not yield any photoluminescence emission, implying efficient non-radiative recombination mechanisms for the charge-carriers. Using λex ∼375 nm, surface fluorophores as well as low-lying edge-states are excited, producing emission over a broad range. However, the emission characteristics vary strongly depending on the pH condition and the choice of excitation wavelength. In particular, the edge state emission is abruptly extinguished at pH ≥ 5, which also marks the onset of deprotonation of surface-bound -COOH groups of the CDs, indicating that the -COO- anionic centers promote quenching of edge-state emission. Using λex ∼450 nm, only the surface fluorophores are excited. The deprotonation of phenolic -OH group at pH > 9 causes a sharp red-shift in the emission spectra at all excitation wavelengths. The pH-sensitive emission behavior of the CDs enable them to serve as optical pH sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising candidates for applications in fields like energy conversion [1], sensors [2,3], biomedical imaging, optoelectronic devices etc. [4–13], primarily due to their remarkable optical properties, which have some similarity with those of other carbon nanomaterials like graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots. In terms of synthesis, both top-down [14,15] or bottom-up [9,10] techniques exist for CDs using a large variety of precursors. In recent times, green synthesis techniques have also been developed [16,17]. Surface modification of synthesized CDs has been found to improve useful properties like water solubility and photoluminescence quantum yield [4,18,19]. Introduction of heteroatoms also produces remarkable variations in the optical properties of CDs, as has been found in case of N-doped [20–23] and S, N co-doped CDs [20,24–26].

In terms of structure, several distinct regions are identifiable in typical CDs [20]. At the centre is the pure carbon core comprising predominantly of graphene-like sp2-hybridized C atoms. The edge of the core, comprising of heteroatomic functional groups, constitutes the edge-state. Apart from the core and the edge, the CDs surface constitutes a third distinct region, replete with small aromatic molecular units, behaving like typical organic dyes. These are essentially residual moieties attached to the outer surface of the carbon core when it is formed via carbonization and dehydration of the precursor molecules.

The different regions of the CDs each have a characteristic contribution towards its overall spectral properties [19,20,27–31]. Starting with the core, optical excitation of sp2-hybridized C matrix induces ππ*-like transitions across the band-gap. Similar ππ* as well as nπ* transitions also occur in the edge-states owing to the abundance of functional groups. These transitions are manifested as either the core absorption band (usually, at λ < 300 nm), or the edge-state absorption band (λ ∼ 300–400 nm) [20]. In both cases, optical excitation produces hole-electron excitons which eventually decay via charge-carrier recombination. Photoluminescence is emitted when the decay follows a radiative pathway. Incidentally, the recombination of charge-carriers can take place both inside the carbon core, or after they have diffused into the edge or surface region, where they may encounter defect states [18,31–34]. All of these factors critically govern the nature of the emission property of CDs.

Besides the excitonic transitions in the core and in the edge of the CDs, the surface fluorophores, i.e., surface-bound small molecule-like units also have their separate photophysics. They usually absorb at λ > 400 nm, and may also display photoluminescence emission just as typical fluorescent aromatic molecules. These units are characterized as surface fluorophores [18,20,35,36]. In general, surface-bound molecular fluorophores contain different functional groups which make them soluble in water. Depending upon the precursor molecule, aromatic molecular units may contain -CO, -OH, -COOH, thiol, amine like groups.

In recent times, a new aspect has emerged in the emission of water soluble CDs: its dependence on pH [36–41]. Since the surface fluorophores of a CD often contain acidic/basic functional groups such as –OH, -COOH, -NH2, etc., variation of pH changes their state of protonation, which may modulate their emission properties. Thus, changes in the emission peak position and quantum yield have been reported for CDs as a function of pH, anticipating their usage as pH sensors. However, there are extremely few works that have attempted to elucidate the mechanism of CDs emission as a consequence of pH change. In a recent femtosecond transient absorption study [36] on CDs at different pH, the authors have identified four different time-regimes representing different events in the overall electronic relaxation process: i) thermalization of initially excited charge-carriers via electron-electron scattering and surface trapping within ∼100 fs; ii) phonon-mediated charge-carrier relaxation occurring in a few picoseconds; iii) relaxation via acoustic phonon scattering in several 10 picoseconds; and finally, iv) hole-electron recombination at the surface trap states, occurring over several 100 ps upto the nanosecond time-scale. They claimed that deprotonation of surface carboxylic groups at alkaline pH significantly accelerates the initial sub-picosecond electron-trapping step, leading to higher quantum yield of the CDs at alkaline pH.

In the present work, we have taken up the spectral study of water-soluble green photoluminescent CDs whose spectral characteristics changes with pH due to the presence of acidic groups at the surface. These were synthesized following a reported procedure [39]. Photoluminescence spectra of the CDs were recorded using several excitation wavelengths covering the entire range from core absorption to surface fluorophore absorption. The method of Time-Resolved Area Normalized Emission Spectra (TRANES) was employed to distinguish between different emitting species in the excited state in solutions of various pH. Our aim was to address the excited state dynamics of the CDs in different pH solutions in the ≥100 ps time-scale. This time-regime is essentially dominated by charge-carrier recombination in the edge-states and the surface fluorophores, as well as by the molecule-like photoluminescence of the latter.

l-ascorbic acid (AA) and Cu(OAc)2.H2O were purchased from SRL and Merck respectively. In a typical experiment, 0.3522 g ascorbic acid (AA) was dissolved in 38 mL water and then 2 mL 0.1 M Cu(OAc)2.H2O solution was added. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 10 min, and then refluxed at ∼90 °C for 5 h. The resulting product was cooled to room temperature and purified by dialyzing against large volume of water followed by centrifugation at 10000 rpm to retain the supernatant. The golden yellow coloured supernatant obtained after centrifugation indicated the formation of CDs. This as-prepared CD solution was green photoluminescent under UV-light (λ = 366 nm), and had pH ∼ 2.25. A series of different solutions with pH = 4, 5, 7, 9 and 11 were prepared from the as-prepared solution, by adding different quantities of NaOH solution to it. All pH solutions were stable and did not show signs of precipitation or coagulation.

Absorption and photoluminescence spectra were recorded using a HITACHI UV spectrophotometer (U-3501) and Perkin Elmer (LS 55) spectrofluorimeter respectively. Picosecond photoluminescence dynamic studies of the fluorophore solutions was performed using a time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) system by employing a picosecond diode laser operating at λex = 375 nm and 450 nm, and a pulsewidth of ∼60 ps. The shape and size of the as prepared CDs were characterized in Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), conducted in a 200 kV instrument (JEOL JEM 2100 HR) at the Centre for Research in Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (CRNN), University of Calcutta. FTIR spectra were recorded using a Perkin–Elmer RXI FTIR spectrophotometer (400–4000 cm−1).

TEM image of CDs presented in Fig. 1 reveal that the CDs are nearly spherical in morphology. HRTEM image (inset figure) reveals that as formed CDs are graphitic in nature with lattice spacing of 0.22 nm and 0.32 nm correspond to (100) and (002) plane of graphite crystal [42–44]. The size distribution curve determines average particle size ∼2.7 nm.

FTIR spectra (Figure SF1 in Supporting Information) of as prepared CDs (pH ∼ 2.25) was collected and it shows two bands at ∼ 1650 cm−1 and ∼3400 cm−1 due to presence of O-H and C=O bonds respectively. Hence IR spectroscopic data clearly indicate the abundance of functional groups appearing on CDs surface.

Photoluminescence emission, excitation and absorption spectra of the CDs in different pH solutions are displayed in Fig. 2 . The absorption spectrum at all pH has a broad, prominent peak at < 300 nm, that may be ascribed to ππ∗ -like transition in the core. It also features a very broad but weak shoulder starting from ∼300 nm and extending far into the region of visible wavelengths. Following our discussion in the Introduction section, this broad band is attributable to edge-state (λ ∼ 300–400 nm) and surface fluorophore (λ > 400 nm).

Photoluminescence emission spectra were recorded for each of the CDs solutions by using excitation wavelengths over the range of absorption band from λex ∼300 nm (i.e., core excitation) to λex ≥ 450 nm (i.e., surface fluorophore excitation). Surprisingly, the CDs solutions were almost non-emissive with λex < 370 nm. At pH > 7, even emission with λex = 370 nm is extremely weak. In other words, excitation of the core, and a substantial portion of edge-states in the high-energy end, produces charge carriers that preferentially undergo non-radiative relaxation, and this becomes more facile at alkaline pH. At alkaline pH, acidic groups at the CDs surface are deprotonated, creating an abundance of anionic sites which appear to promote non-radiative charge-carrier recombination.

The normalized photoluminescence emission spectra of the CDs solutions recorded with λex ≥ 370 nm are appended to Fig. 2. The condition λex ≥ 370 nm implies that surface fluorophores and low-energy edge states are preferentially excited. We note that for any CDs solution of a given pH, the emission spectra undergo red-shift as the excitation wavelength is red-shifted. This red-shift of the emission spectra is a well-known feature of CD photoluminescence, and indicates the heterogeneous nature of surface fluorophore emission. This has been attributed principally to the in-band ground-state heterogeneity [34].

Excitation spectra of the CDs solutions were recorded at emission wavelengths corresponding to the emission peaks. Some of the representative normalized excitation spectra of CDs solutions at all pH are appended to Fig. 2. We note that, although the absorption spectrum of the CDs solution at any pH is extremely broad, the excitation spectra are quite narrow, occupying only a limited wavelength range mostly between 370 nm and 470 nm. Thus, the emission response of the CDs solution derives no contribution from the excitation of the core or the high energy edge-states. Moreover, the excitation spectra exhibit dependence on emission wavelength (λem). As the λem is red-shifted, the excitation spectral peaks also get red-shifted. This reflects the same shifting behavior as the emission spectra (described above) and thus reinforces the picture that the surface fluorophore population is composed of sub-sets of moieties with different characteristic absorption or emission peaks.

From Fig. 2, it is evident that the emission spectra of the CDs solutions undergo an overall red-shift as the pH is changed from acidic to alkaline. To examine this shift closely, we plotted the emission peak positions as a function of pH in Fig. 3 . We chose the peaks of emission spectra obtained with λex = 400 nm, 450 nm and 520 nm, which largely covers the range of excitation wavelength used. In Fig. 3, we note a brake for peaks at three λex between pH = 9 and 11. Another less abrupt brake is found for peaks with λex = 400 nm and 450 nm, between pH = 2 and 5. This suggests that deprotonation involves two distinct sets of proton-donor units: more acidic -COOH groups respond at lower pH (<5), while less acidic phenolic -OH groups respond at higher pH (∼11).

Emission time-profiles of the CDs solutions were recorded using excitation wavelengths 375 nm and 450 nm, ensuring preferential excitation of the edge-states and surface flurophores, respectively. In each case, emission was collected at several emission wavelengths spanning nearly the entire range of emission spectra. A few representative time-profiles are displayed in Fig. 4 , where certain common features are noticeable among all of them. Firstly, each of the time-profiles exhibit multi-exponential decay behavior. Secondly, the decays become faster at longer emission wavelengths, irrespective of solution pH and choice of excitation wavelength. Thirdly, none of the time-profiles have any growth-like feature.

All the time-profile curves were fitted using multi-exponential decay function:
F(t) = Ʃ ai exp (-t/τi)

The detailed fitting parameters are listed in Table ST1, ST2, ST3, ST4 in the Supporting Information Section. Here, we would like to point out that the τi values listed in these tables (obtained by fitting the time-profile curves with multi-exponential decay functions) should be considered essentially as mere fitting parameters, since we do not have adequate supporting evidence to attach any physical significance to their specific numerical values.
Using these fitting parameters, the time-resolved area normalized emission spectra (TRANES) was constructed at both λex = 375 nm and 450 nm for each pH solutions.

The construction of TRANES occurs in two steps. First, using the fitting parameters, the time-resolved emission spectra (TRES) for a given pH solution and a given excitation wavelength were constructed. At any arbitrary time-delay t = t′, the TRES intensity at an emission energy E = Ei is given by equation 1:
I
( E =
E
i
, t = t ' )
= I
(
E =
E
i
, t = 0
)
×
{
a
1
e x p
(
−
t '
τ
1
)
+
a
2
e x p
(
−
t '
τ
2
)
+
a
3
e x p
(
−
t '
τ
3
)
}
where
I(E = Ei,t = 0) = Isteady-state(E = Ei)/{a1τ1 + a2τ2+ a3τ3}, and
Isteady-state(E = Ei) is the steady-state spectral intensity at the emission energy E = Ei.

Once a TRES is constructed, the corresponding TRANES is obtained from it in the second step, by dividing the TRES curve by the area covered by it. The TRANES curves for the CD solutions of different pH are displayed in Fig. 5 .

The TRANES method has been widely and successfully used in determining the number and distribution of emitting species in the time-resolved photoluminescence response of a system [45,46]. If it is found that TRANES curves constructed at different time-delays and plotted together intersect at a unique isoemissive point, then it can be proved that the emitting system contains two emissive species in the excited state, irrespective of whether they are in equilibrium or not. In fact, the great advantage of the TRANES method is that this conclusion can be drawn without taking recourse to any pre-conceived notion about the nature and composition of the system concerned [45,46]. Keeping these in mind, we note that a single isoemissive point exists for each TRANES curve at all pH for λex = 450 nm, and at all pH except pH = 2 for λex = 375 nm over the time-delay of 100 ps to 10 ns. The position of isoemissive point in each case is noted and plotted against pH in Fig. 3.

The situation may be explained with the help of a simplified diagram as in Fig. 6 . With λex = 450 nm, only the surface fluorophores are excited. Evidently, this leads to the emergence of two emitting species in the excited state at all pH. The isoemissive point remains nearly same between pH = 2 and pH = 9, after which it sharply red-shifts at pH = 11. This closely reflects the behavior of the steady-state emission peaks recorded with λex = 450 nm. Hence, deprotonation of the phenolic -OH groups definitely affects the emission properties of the surface fluorophores.

With λex = 375 nm too, the presence of two emissive species at all pH > 2 is revealed by the occurrence of a single isoemissive point. In addition, the same sharp red-shift in the isoemissive point is observed between pH = 9 and pH = 11. However, at pH = 2, the TRANES curves exhibit 2 isoemissive points instead of 1 - a low energy point at 19200 cm−1 during early time-delays, and a high-energy point at 20900 cm−1 for time-delays ≥ 5 ns. This feature strongly suggests the involvement of more than 2 emissive species in the excited state [43]. Also, the sharp blue-shift of the long-time isoemissive point reflects the brake in the emission peak position obtained with λex = 400 nm and at pH < 5.

With λex = 375 nm, a section of high-energy edge-states are also excited, so that the emission includes contributions from both edge-states as well as surface fluorophores. The un-ionized COOH groups at pH = 2 are extensively deprotonated at pH ≥ 5, producing anionic centers in the form of -COO- groups. In the previous section, we had argued that the spectral results indicate that these anionic groups promote the non-radiative recombination of charge-carriers. Hence at pH ≥ 5, the edge-state emission is severely quenched, and the emission we obtain with λex = 375 nm is overwhelmingly due to the surface fluorophores. Evidently, the emission of surface fluorophores bearing -COOH and -COO- groups do not differ substantially, which explains the absence of a sharp brake in the positions of either the emission peaks or isoemissive points between pH = 2 and pH = 5, when excited at λex ≥ 450 nm.

The CDs used in this work were prepared with ascorbic acid as precursor molecule and possess surface-bound acidic functional groups like -COOH and phenolic –OH. We found that the overall spectral response of the CDs depends on the solution pH, an occurrence that illustrates the deep connection between their photophysics and the state of protonation of their surface acid groups. As mentioned earlier, optical excitation of the CDs can be broadly divided into three regimes: i) the pure carbon core at λex < 300 nm, ii) the edge-states at λex = 300–400 nm, and iii) the surface fluorophores at λex > 400 nm. In case of our CDs, we found hardly any photoluminescence emission for λex < 370 nm, although they all have quite high absorbance in that wavelength range. This implies that a substantial number of charge-carriers produced by excitation of the core and edge-states undergo nonradiative decay, irrespective of solution pH.

Using λex = 450–500 nm, we found strong photoluminescence emission, which obviously originated from the surface fluorophores of the CDs. Here, the emission peak position registers abrupt red-shift at two pH ranges: a minor shift at pH < 5 and a major shift at pH > 9. These two pH regions nearly coincide with the typical pKa range of –COOH and phenolic –OH groups, respectively. In other words, the emission peak red-shifts at pH < 5 and at pH > 9 reflect the sudden deprotonation of the –COOH and phenolic –OH groups respectively. The effect of the latter is stronger.

The range λex = 370–400 nm covers the intermediate energy window that predominantly excites low-energy edge-states. This generates charge-carriers that may undergo radiative recombination locally, giving edge-state emission at shorter wavelengths, or transfer energy to the surface fluorophores producing surface emission at relatively longer wavelengths. This accounts for the broad emission spectrum obtained with λex = 370–400 nm at pH = 2. However, as the pH is raised to 5 and 7, the emission spectrum with λex = 370–400 nm is drastically narrowed down because its short wavelength end is quenched (see Fig. 2). Since the –COOH groups are deprotonated at the same pH range, this indicates that the emerging –COO- groups promote efficient nonradiative relaxation for charge-carriers, quenching the edge-state emission while leaving the surface emission unaffected. This also explains the sudden red-shift in emission peak position with λex = 400 between pH = 2 and 5, as depicted in Fig. 3. In fact, at still more alkaline pH (i.e. pH > 7), we do not obtain any photoluminescence emission at all with λex = 370 nm, showing how the proliferation of anionic carboxylate and phenolate groups at the surface nearly extinguishes emission from the edge-states.

Based on time-resolved spectroscopic results TRANES curves were constructed. With λex = 375 nm, the TRANES curves possess unique isoemissive points at all pH except only pH = 2. At pH = 2 the absence of unique isoemissive point across the entire time scale is attributed to a large degree of heterogeneity in the emission response, which originates from both the edge as well as the surface states. In particular, the TRANES at pH = 2 displays with two isoemissive points at two different time intervals. The low-energy isoemissive point at shorter time-delays lies in the same wavelength range as those of the TRANES recorded for solutions of pH > 2. On the other hand, the high energy isoemissive point at longer time-delays is unique for pH = 2. The latter disappears in solutions of higher pH, where deprotonation of acidic groups creates anionic sites facilitating non-radiative relaxation of the charge-carriers responsible for edge state emission.

Last but not least, the shift in the photoluminescence emission peak position as a function of pH means that the CDs can serve as potential optical pH sensors. According to Fig. 3, the pH-sensitivity is especially strong with λex > 400 nm, and around pH = 5 and pH = 9, where the emission peak shift is noticeably sharp.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support of this work was obtained from DST-SERB, India (Project No. EMR/2017/000387), and CSIR, India (Project No. 01(2390)/10/EMR – II). Time-resolved picosecond spectroscopic experiments were carried out at the University of Calcutta with the TCSPC instrument purchased under the DST (FIST Program), India. N. B. thanks University Grants Commission, India, for awarding research fellowship.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.12.056.
